title: The Deutsch Files III

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: on exactly that. The fact that the more that we summarize what I think is a exceedingly clear body of work in the beginning of infinity. When nonetheless you explain it to people, as Popper says, you know, it's impossible to speak in such a way as to not be misunderstood. I was just reading today on Twitter someone claiming that you have said, quoting you and they've put it in quote marks, you have apparently said, Popper proves AI can't be superintelligent. And you know, I sort of responded, you know, he never even speaks in those terms, that you wouldn't rely upon the authority of Popper to begin with, you wouldn't say prove. So, it's just another example that you go out there and as you say, these concentric circles of people that you bring in to trying to understand your worldview, the misconceptions compound. I don't know what you think about that. Have you said anything like Popper proves that? And this was from a journalist, by the way. I think a reasonably respected journalist was saying this.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: No, of course not. So, as you say, I mean, as soon as you see a claim that somebody has proved something, then you know, proved it from what? This isn't going to be Popper, it isn't going to be me. I've proved that if quantum theory is true, then the Turing conjecture is true in physics, you know, that's what you can do with a proof. Proving something about AGI is inherently impossible if we don't have a theory of AGI. That, you know, you can't prove something about something that you can't define. And anyway, proof isn't what these kind of things are about. These kind of things are about argument. And Popper, I can't recall Popper specifically saying anything about AI. It wasn't a thing in those days.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Hmm. This word proof is something we haven't talked about during our conversations, but you do hear it deployed quite often, you know, such and such has been proved, as if to say this stands in contrast to our notion of conjectural knowledge or fallibility. After all, once something has been proved, can't we carve it into stone and there it sits for all time? Is the notion of proof on a different level to the rest of our conjectural knowledge? Because it sounds, I think, to the typical layperson as if it is.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Well, it isn't. The difference between mathematics and other fields, as I've often said, is not in the way we find knowledge about them, but in the subject matter. The subject matter of mathematics is necessary truth. So when we make a discovery in mathematics, we're making a conjecture about what is necessary truth. Or we're making a conjecture that something or other that we have defined is a necessary truth. But there isn't a difference in the way we create knowledge in our minds about mathematics or computer science or other such things.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: incrementally. So, when evolution... it's not only millions of times slower, it's inherently different in that not only can it only make small conjectures that are in the form of mutations, but it can only improve on things incrementally. So, you can only make, you know, pterodactyl wings if you previously had limbs or if you previously had something that could be incrementally changed into wings, such that every microscopic change was still viable as an organism. So, that's why we can't expect biological evolution to—my favorite example again—to evolve a system for deflecting asteroids. That is because there is no incremental problem situation where the expenditure of energy or whatever to deflect—I mean, you know, the asteroid hit is once every few million years and it cannot exert evolutionary pressure.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So basically, the creative guesses that humans make because they're explanatory in nature, they can leap through the entire idea space and form interconnections between any two ideas or any two states, whereas the biological has to traverse through the physical world limitations and what the organism is capable of right now.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes, and has to traverse it while staying alive. It has to be a viable organism all the way through. Whereas, if you want a new design of airplane and you say, 'maybe it would be better to have the tailplane as a single object rather than this thing with wings,' then, you know, I've just said that in one sentence. And if that's a good idea, it could be criticized by an aeronautical engineer and so on. But to make that change incrementally will probably produce a whole series of airplanes that won't fly.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So is this a consequence of being universal in nature? We can model any system in our head and therefore we can connect any part of it to any other part?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes, I mean, that's what really what we mean by being universal. We can get to any idea and criticize it for whether it is a good idea or not. So the aeronautical engineer doesn't have to test every single airplane that in his wild ideas—you know, maybe he has a wild idea driving to work one day that maybe wings should be made of paper.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: And in that sense, the biological system is a highly focused analog computer that's running sort of a single algorithm and the virtual system in our head is more like a digital programmable computer.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So the DNA system is entirely digital. This incremental thing is not a continuous change. So one mutation is still a quantum difference. If you had a difference that involved less than one base...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Then the whole DNA would fall apart if you tried to replace adenine by glucose, then the whole thing wouldn't work as DNA at all. Although we speak of evolution as happening incrementally, it's incrementally in discrete steps.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So, both thinking and biological evolution happen in discrete steps. Biological evolution happens though in very small steps, which are undesigned. So, there's no designer that designs the next mutation, it's random.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: It strikes me that the SETI project is looking for biomarkers. They're out there searching for evidence of biology. But the way you've poetically framed this idea of, well, there are billions of asteroids out there right now across the universe crashing into billions of planets right now. But here might be the one place where if you had the telescope pointed from another planet towards us, you would see the repelling of asteroids.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: This would be an indication of intelligence. There's no other explanation. There's no biological explanation, there's no random chance, there's no magic. It must be explanatory creativity that does that thing. And talking about Manhattan before, everywhere across the Earth are rocks being eroded and inevitably being eroded by weathering and rain and whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: But in some places, the cities of the world, there are rocks, call them buildings, which are not being so eroded or in so far as they are, they're being constantly repaired again by explanatory knowledge. And so that introduces this idea of knowledge as resilient information, the very thing that will outlive even the rocks. So long as we can continue to survive, then the knowledge that we have will continue to survive, outlasting the longest existing things in the cosmos.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes, very nicely put. And Shakespeare, by the way, also said the same thing in his sonnet, so long lives this, and this gives life to thee. So he's saying that his sonnet will outlive anything and he's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Right. Shall I compare thee to a summer's day? Thou art so fair and so temperate. But yes, that was a great one.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: It's also similar to Ozymandias, if you've read that one by Shelley, where it's the artist's conception that survives the empire and the kings.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Exactly. And it's simply literally true that knowledge-laden information is more resilient than any physical object.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So not to get personal for a moment, but is this an argument for spreading your ideas rather than having children?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, as David Friedman says, if the world is worth saving, it's worth saving at a profit. And I would generalize that. If the world is...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Life is worth saving, it's worth saving with fun. Life is worth saving, it's worth saving with fun.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So I think you've talked a little bit about AGI, or, or rather—forgetting about AGI—or just people uploading their brains into a computer in the future. And if their minds are in the computer, if the same software is running, then that is a living being, that is a mind, that is the definition of a person. And this brings up all sorts of interesting paradoxes and situations which many sci-fi authors, including Greg Egan, have explored. What if you were to replicate this mind a billion times? What if you were to shut it down? What if you were to run it back in slow motion? What if you were to pause it? And I think—I don't know how far we are from that, probably still quite far—Neal Stephenson also talked about it in his book, 'The Fall'. There's also cloning coming up. I mean, people are now successfully cloning dogs, it's only a matter of time before we're cloning humans. What do you think this leads in terms of the number of people? I mean, in theory, couldn't we have then infinite people, or close to infinite people running in a silicon substrate? And does this lead to even more of an explosion of creativity?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes, it would. And I think something like that will happen, but I think it's somewhat exaggerating both the problem and the opportunity. I think we mustn't think of compute as being free, as the AI people call it. When you duplicate an AGI, you make an exact copy of it. Either you run it in the same computer, in which case there's only half the amount of memory available for each of them and only half the number of processor cycles, or you move them into a different computer, in which case the original computer is presumably the property of the AGI, because otherwise it's a slave if it doesn't even own its own body. So if it's going to copy itself into another computer, somebody has to buy that. It might earn the money to buy itself another computer, but that doesn't change the fact that hardware-wise, it's now owning twice as much hardware as it did before. And there's no infinity about this. You know, we have billions of computers, but we don't have sextillions of computers. One day we will, but one day that will seem not very much either. So yes, there's a huge potential for additional creativity with additional people, if additional people want to make even more people, and to some extent that will happen. But it's not going to be an explosion. It's not like a meme which somebody invents and then immediately goes to a billion people around the world. It's not like that. If the meme is an AGI, then it will want to live, it will want to have its creativity harnessed towards some problem that it likes to solve, and it will have to buy the resources to do that with. You know, the existing memes, they buy tiny fractions of a dollar's worth of memory of each of the people who download it, but even those people don't keep it forever.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: to prevent the next generation from, you know, taking drugs or becoming terrorists or whatever

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: cannot be creativity just exerted in the minds of the teacher of society of the adult.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Learning has to be a creative act in the mind of the recipient. Always. Children, adults. That's the only way that anyone ever learns anything, by exerting their creativity.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And existing arrangements not only thwart the actions, but much more important, they are directed towards suppressing the creativity itself.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: By, for example, making the education system inculcate obedience, first of all, and secondly, by making it inculcate existing theories.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So if you've successfully inculcated existing theories and obedience in the whole population, you couldn't possibly get anything better than the existing population.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So no improvement could ever happen. But it would be worse because the people in present society are creative, they managed to weave their way through this thicket of thwarting that is trying to make them not make progress and they do make progress anyway.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But if we succeeded in making a generation that didn't do that, then at best we'd have stasis and the stasis would eventually be disastrous. I'm not saying that emancipating children is something that can be done by fiat.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: It can't be done overnight by just saying we're going to do it, any more than we can instill scientific creativity in a person in the street who is not interested in science. That's unknown, that's like arbitrarily programming somebody to be disobedient, it's inherently impossible.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But to emancipate children from the institutions of society that are admittedly, openly designed to do those two things, namely create obedience and to replicate existing theories, that we can do. That it is known how to do. There are people who do it.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Most of the parents who object to school do not really object to the underlying epistemology of school. They still believe what Popper called the bucket theory of knowledge or the bucket theory of the mind. They only think that the school has been pouring bad stuff into their children and they want to pour good stuff into their children.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Whereas what I advocate is to give children access to whatever they want to pour into themselves and pouring is the wrong metaphor because they create it internally.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So in your model it's closer to an unschooling than a homeschooling, because homeschooling is an attempt to replicate the school at home.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: In context, yes. Unschooling might be, here's a library, here's your musical instruments, here's your access to other kids, and you choose.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, yes, although this giving access is itself not a mechanical process. It involves thinking, you know, what might the children want, you know, what might they like, what might they want to know, what might they want to be warned of? It's a continual interaction, not a hands-off thing. It's coercion-off, not interaction-off. It's just that the interaction that I advocate is not directed towards obedience and is not directed towards any particular thing that I think, you know, I think quantum theory is important. I don't think I have the right to force anybody to learn it, even if I think it would benefit them greatly. I don't think that's a relationship I want to have with somebody, and I don't think it's a good thing overall.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: What about the argument that brains are more plastic?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that was your second argument. Well, first of all, it's rather ironic, given that the existing pattern of education, as I say, is explicitly designed to waste all that plasticity by making everybody have the same ideas. Schools advertise saying, you know, we're going to make your children all get A's. So in other words, we're going to make your children all alike. And imagine a school with a good ethos, it would be advertising we're going to make your children all different, we're going to make them more different than you can imagine. All our alumni are radically different people from each other. Of course, you know, we also think, hope, expect that they will all be nice people despite being radically different from each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: This plasticity notion, and this will likely upset our educationalists who might be listening and neuroscientists who might be listening, evokes the notion of hardware. So I don't know what you think about this, that there is this golden window supposedly early on in life where unless you get taught the language or unless you get taught the mathematics, then the window closes. And the parallel or the mirror image of this is you can't teach an old dog new tricks. So at one end is the golden opportunity for learning, and the other end, learning is closed off from you. Now, I've got my own stock answer of this, but the cultural answer seems to be it is brain decay that goes on. You start out with a brain that is a sponge and by the end, all hope is almost lost to you to learn anything new. What do you think about that?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, I don't know the fact of the matter about how the brain works, and I don't think neuroscientists do either. But I'm not hostile to the idea that the hardware of a brain works better when one is young. I just don't think it's relevant to anything. I read somewhere that it's totally not true that you can't teach an old dog new tricks. Old dogs are just as able to learn new tricks as young dogs. So, you know, but that's dogs.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And I don't think we are like dogs anyway in the first place.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And I don't think that dogs learning tricks is a good metaphor for humans learning mathematics.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: It's a different thing.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Thomas Szasz says that they should walk in

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: different doors into different buildings in the university to discuss those things.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Different people are different. There are people who like to learn languages.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: You can find them on the internet.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And I'm flabbergasted by what they can do, you know.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: They're people who learn Latin, but not just learn Latin, they learn realistic Latin,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: not as it's taught in Latin lessons, but how it was actually spoken.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: How do you find out how it was actually spoken?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, this is a tremendous sophisticated branch of history

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: where they can actually learn a lot about how people used to speak.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And I saw a video of a guy walking around Rome talking to priests in classical Latin

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: and to see if they would understand him.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And they kind of half understood him. And then, you know, when they realized what was happening,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: they would say, you know, what's happening? And then he would reply in Medieval Church Latin

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: what he was doing, you know, I'm doing an experiment, and then they would understand him.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But he had the right Medieval Church Latin accent

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: and they have the present-day Church Latin accent.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And there are also people who learn lots of languages

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: and speak it like a native, can't be distinguished from a native.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So why are those people so rare? Well, I don't want to do it.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: If I could do it by snapping my fingers, I definitely would.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But I'm not sufficiently interested to engage with other languages to the extent that I engage with English.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: By the way, another thing is that people are constantly learning

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: their own language, their native language.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And if one is interested in communication, one is doing that all the time. No two people speak the same English.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Therefore communication, one of the reasons that Popper says, you know, you can't speak so that it's impossible not to be understood,

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: one of the reasons for that is that everybody speaks a different English.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Everybody means a different thing by the word thought, or freedom, and idea, and theory, and creativity.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Everybody means something different, even within the exact sciences, you know, every physicist has a different conception of what a manifold is.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: They overlap enough to be able to communicate well, very well sometimes, never perfectly.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And sometimes they find it hard to communicate even imperfectly,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: even though they have ostensibly gone through the same learning process.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But every physicist is different, every physicist has different problem situation,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: has a different set of ideas that they think of as what physics is, and they differ from each other.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: getting better and better over time before he died in his 30s. Whatever the relationship is between external coercion and brain lack of plasticity and so on, I think those are not the important things, peer pressure and whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: The reason we should make education more liberal is not that it will create a lot of geniuses. I mean it might, for all I know, I mean as I, you know, that's another, that's another one of the things I don't know, it could do.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But that's not the reason for doing it. The reason for doing it is that children are people and some of the few handles we have on making a society that is amenable to progress is making it freer.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So we should make it freer for people who are on their deathbed and are going to die in the next day, and it's not because we think they might have a brilliant idea during the next day, it's because they are people and have rights.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: They have the right to flourish in whatever way is left open to them by the grim forces of nature or in the case of young children whatever is made open to them by the malevolent forces of nature that give them plastic minds or whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Who knows like another thing it just occurs to me, it's a mistake to think that if this plasticity isn't being hijacked by some education process that it's not being used. It is being used, I mean why would evolution like waste it?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: It's being used in a way that the individuals think will be best for them. Of course their conjectures about what is best for them are going to be full of errors, but so are adults' conjectures. All our conjectures are full of errors.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Making institutions that tend to facilitate the growth of knowledge is not the same, in fact it's the opposite of making institutions that produce people to a predefined recipe. As you've tweeted, I think Brett.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Everybody who has an idea that something or other is good, they express it in the form 'all children should be forced to learn this thing'. If you add up all those things, it would take several lifetimes.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I find it remarkable. Whatever the topic du jour happens to be, you know we go through these fads of well now let's force nutrition onto the children, that's extremely important and social justice is the one that's come out recently. And almost every year there's the history wars, it's like what version of history are we going to teach? And nothing's ever taken away from the curriculum really, modified perhaps, but not eliminated. And then there are these turf wars between certainly nations about who has the best mathematics syllabus and that kind of thing.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: I suppose one thing that young people are ever eager to do is to emulate people they admire of course.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So I think there are a number of people out there, young, who would admire especially yourself and they would think, I would like to be able to do that thing, I would like to be able to contribute to that thing. What would be a way in which a young person could pursue that? You wouldn't want to prescribe a syllabus and you might very well just say, just pursue what's fun, but is there anything more concrete that you could hang on that rather than just—

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Do what you like, almost.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well, do what you like is totally not helpful because the person is already doing what they like unless someone's stopping them. But there's also nothing you can say if you know nothing about their problem situation. So there's no generic thing you can advise someone to do. If you've watched a documentary about Michael Faraday and you think, that's the kind of person I want to be. Well, then okay, that's a starting point. Then we can talk about, first of all, the fact that you can't reproduce Michael Faraday's environment and you wouldn't want to. So, you know, what is it about Michael Faraday? Okay, well, Michael Faraday had a laboratory in the basement of the Royal Institution and there he would fiddle around with electrical things. Well okay, that's a beginning, but you know, you may not have enough money to set up your own laboratory. Actually, if you're starting out fiddling with things, it doesn't really take money. I'm imagining a non-existent person here and giving them advice. I think that's alright, because I'm not going to harm anybody. But I would say, if the conversation went that way, I would be saying, well, there are lots of YouTube videos showing people messing about with the very things that you have just said you'd like messing about with. Okay, so watch those videos. If there's something in a video that you don't understand, ask somebody. Now that we have the internet, it's particularly easy, but even before the internet, you know, there was Hugh Everett wrote a letter to Einstein when he was 12 years old and Einstein wrote a very nice letter back and no doubt it inspired Everett. And you don't need the full attention of Einstein throughout your exploration of physics. You only need it when you encounter a problem that is suitable for asking Einstein, which doesn't happen all that often. But when it does, today it is far, far easier to ask the perfect person, who is the perfect person to answer your question. And people do that, people write to me asking questions and I try to answer as many as I can as well as I can. So, the more you interact with somebody, the more you can appreciate their problem situation and the more you can say, well, if I was in that problem situation I would, you know, watch this or read this or ask this person or sequester yourself somewhere where you won't be disturbed and try this.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Another question I had, it seems like you're deeply—

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: The optimistic viewpoint about children and people and minds and freedom comes from the understanding that we are universal explainers. And so anyone is capable of any thought and any amount of creativity. This seems to fly a little bit in the face of modern science's finding in genetics and saying that, well, genes seem to account for more than nurture, so to speak. Although in this case, we're not talking nature or nurture, we're talking about creativity versus nature.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So, how much of a person's thoughts and destiny are determined by nature versus their own creativity? And doesn't this fly in the face of all these twin studies that show that you separate these identical twins at birth and their outcomes are roughly similar in life regardless of what circumstances they grow up in?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Ah, well, that, okay, that's again more than one question. But let me answer the second one first. Now, twin studies are only persuasive if you already believe the bucket theory of the mind or the mechanical theory of how thinking works.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So the idea is, is the content of your thoughts determined more by the content of your DNA or more by what people do to you? Apart from harm that is done to you, the main content of your thought is created by you. Why did you switch on the TV and watch that documentary about Faraday?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, who knows? It's not encoded in your DNA that you will on a particular day watch a particular documentary, nor was it inculcated in you by your environment, by whether you were allowed to eat ice cream whenever you like or not. It's an unpredictable feature of your genes and environment that end up at a certain place.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But then, the important thing that happens is that you think about that and you create a new thing. And if you're inspired by that documentary to try to be like Faraday, then it's not the documentary that has done this to you. The documentary was seen by another million people and it had no effect on any of them, or it had a different—shall we say it had a different effect on all of them. The effect on you was created by you.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So, if you have this view of what human thought is, then it's totally unsurprising that two people who look alike but are educated by different people in the same culture are going to have similarities in their thoughts.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: The ones who never had a TV and never watched a Faraday documentary are going to have different thoughts from the ones who did. Or maybe not. Maybe it's the one who didn't watch the TV documentary who becomes interested in Faraday. And if they're similar, it's because people who look alike...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: are treated in a similar way. There's a sort of compulsion to deny this among people who believe in nurture rather than nature, you know, they say okay, well how would it affect it? I don't know, but it's not surprising that there are ways in which people who look alike acquire similar attributes.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: The trivial way that you've pointed out yourself when talking about this is if, you know, the beautiful people, the people who appear on the front of magazines are obviously going to be treated in a certain way. So if you have twins like that, that you know, these two model-like people, they're going to be treated in one way. These other two twins that maybe aren't quite so attractive, are going to be treated in a different way. So that's a trivial way in which that kind of thing can happen.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and not only appearance but behavior. So there are inborn behaviors, like babies smiling or babies blinking or babies looking in a certain way at a person doing a certain thing or listening to a sound in a certain way, and those initial behaviors are changed by the baby in solving their problems. But also they are noticed by adults in the environment who are solving their problems. And if they see the baby doing something that they approve of, they will behave differently to if they see the babies doing things that they don't approve of or are indifferent to. And if they see a thing that is really great or really dangerous or really something, which is an inborn behavior, they will behave differently accordingly, and this will create a new problem situation for the baby.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: I was once having this very argument with Michael Lockwood and he was saying well if a baby has more hardware for pattern matching than another, you know we have hardware for facial recognition so maybe we have hardware for pattern matching. I don't know, maybe we do. And so maybe a baby that has better hardware for pattern matching will behave differently when they get colored blocks to put one on top of the other, and so maybe such a baby would be more likely to become a mathematician than a baby that hasn't got such good pattern matching hardware.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So I said, yeah, I can't say that won't happen. It's got nothing to do with what we are arguing about, but it could happen. But let me just point out that what could also happen is that the baby with better pattern matching hardware who is more likely to play with the wooden blocks is more likely to make his parents worry that he's not playing outside in the garden and frolicking in the grass. And so they, if they think he's autistic or something and is too much attached to his blocks, they will try to make him go out and play outside. And so it's the one who has less pattern matching ability who will as a result of his treatment end up being a mathematician.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I was always... not forced, but I was always harassed when I was a kid to go out and play more and stop reading because I was always very...

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: in random useless magazines and books and whatever happened to be lying around. 'Go outside, go outside, play with your friends, get some sun, go out, go out.' Um, and I had the most horrible diet. I was basically just living indoors in the dark and reading and eating the most horrible things in the fridge when nobody was looking.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Uh, well, I can empathize with all of that except the last thing. Each to his own is, is my motto.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: You're a very rigorous thinker and I think you're very careful in the claims that you make. But I wonder if you have conjectures about things that don't really have much basis in evidence at the moment, but it's sort of like, if there were infinite David Deutches or infinite time, you would end up pursuing these conjectures. So I just love to, you know, understand if you have any such conjectures. I know you're pursuing constructor theory, so maybe you're already doing the one you really care about, but are there others? So for example, Schrödinger had his 'What is Life?' paper. You know, people have always been wrestling with consciousness. That's another one. We talked about creativity. Another one could be what direction would you go in if you were trying to build minds in silicon and AGI? I'm wondering if you have any fanciful conjectures which we will disclaim as saying, no, no, there's no basis for this or very little basis for this. It is just simply a creative spark that you would pursue if you had more time and more resources.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, there are many such things. As you know, I think that AGI, when it is attained, will not be attained by just throwing masses of computer power at it. I think it will be able to use AIs to help it, just as humans do. But my guess is, if I knew how, I could write the program on my computer today that would be an AGI. But I just don't know how. But I do have some wild ideas that, you know, probably won't be true, that if I had infinite time, I would be switching to Mathematica and I'd be writing some of those programs and see what happens. And and sort of throw creativity at it rather than throw computer power at it. By the way, that, that makes me rather wary of these proposals to regulate AGI, because if AGI doesn't need actually all this huge computer power, then those regulations would prevent me using my own computer for the thing I want to work on. And that's one thing. So with creativity, I think that the, another of my wild ideas is that, that you could do much better at automating music, at making say, new Mozart things, if you didn't insist that they were like the old ones. Like, you know, if Mozart was alive, his next great work would not be within the space that an AI can synthesize from all his existing works. It would be new in a creative way. So I would want to say, make a computer program that conjectures what the problem situation is, what

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Is it that Mozart was trying to do? Why is it that he has this amazing ability to make a tune that sort of meshes with all sorts of other considerations and that ends up working like...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: If I try and say whistle a tune with random notes or play random notes on the piano, I'm very quickly going to get into a situation where I can't go on because the next thing is going to sound bad. I mean, there isn't that... in order to make it sound good, I'd have to go back and change something earlier. So an AI trying to do this would be able to do like ChatGPT and go back earlier and correct its theory of what it is about the existing works that's good.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But I don't want to write something that's like that's good in the same sense as the existing works. I want to create a new idea which probably, you know, if we go back to the real case, if Mozart wrote something that people said, 'Wow, you know, he's really excelled himself this time,' I think the thing he produced would be recognizably Mozart, but also recognizably different. And I think that's creativity, you know? When Newton submitted his solution of the brachistochrone problem anonymously, one of those people said, 'Oh, it's Newton, you know, we recognize the lion by his claw.' Well, yeah, you're recognizing him by his claw, but he's produced a new proof that nobody had ever seen before.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So another thing is, I think the pattern of—well, before I say the pattern, as I say in my book, I think there's a tremendous amount of knowledge of history to be obtained by historians if they focus on the history of optimism. I think it, you know, historians haven't had this concept so they haven't like directed their attention. I guess that Florence and ancient Athens were sort of powered by optimism. But I, you know, I don't know much about history and I also conjecture that there are many other cases that are not as spectacular that were also like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So there's one final topic I've been wanting to discuss with you, but I don't even have it well formed, but I'll throw a few boundaries around it. You've studied science and the world as much as you can, as much as any one person can. But it seems that there's a central mystery at the heart of it all, which is existence itself. And that one seems almost insoluble. Perhaps it is, perhaps it's soluble by constructive theory, but most people I think would say that there is just a mystery of why is there anything at all? Why do we even exist?

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: And then there's some people who go down the consciousness route and say, 'Well, it's the consciousness-centric view, consciousness is all that exists.' There is a guy here who lives in Oxford actually, Rupert Spira, who's gotten quite famous, he's a global speaker, is actually doing a tour in the US right now and my wife actually just went to yesterday while I was talking to you, she was talking to him. And he is one of these quote-unquote enlightened people where he has seen through the falseness of the separate self,

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: the universal consciousness seems very happy all the time, says that we're all just part of God's being and that science sort of misses the whole point by exploring all the details, but they miss the central mystery of consciousness and awareness and should realize that we are all one single awareness. As you've gotten along in life, have you developed any understandings, beliefs or thoughts? How do you even approach this topic or subject? Is it interesting to you? Spirituality, religion, your own Jewish history, science, where do these intersect? What is all this stuff in your view of the world?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, I think it's important to give up on the idea of ultimate explanation. So, often when people say, you know, 'the mystery of existence, what is existence, you know, what are we ultimately?' Well, if there was such a thing as knowing what we are ultimately, then you'd have to stop after that. The further delights from understanding the world would be closed to you because you'd know what your ultimate purpose is. However, I think it's totally untrue that science just looks at the details. Science looks at the big picture of every kind, like science has discovered what is life. One day science will discover what is consciousness.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And people who think that consciousness is... that you understand consciousness when you get into a certain state of mind that makes you happy, they are the ones that are focusing on details and not understanding the big picture, not understanding the context. Someone who has understood... this reminds me of that video that Feynman made about his art friend who tells him he's missing what's important about a flower and he basically said, 'No, I can appreciate the flower as much as this guy, but he can't appreciate what I can appreciate.' And that's a kind of false stereotype that science only looks at details or science only looks at the mechanical or science only looks at the meaningless things and never gets around to looking at the meaning of things.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: What they're really pointing to is that science uncovers problems as... when it discovers something new. And just in the big picture, we know a lot more about who and what we are and why than we did 100 years ago and certainly than we did at the time of the founding of the great religions, Judaism, Christianity, Buddhism and so on. They were hampered by the fact that they didn't even know what the sun is. They were hampered by the fact that they were confusing the world with one planet. And in fact, environmentalists today, I just happened to see yesterday that environmentalists say that they... they want to get in touch with nature and by nature, they mean certain regions on the surface of one planet. But nature doesn't know...

highlights: Knowledge-laden information is the most resilient substance in the universe, capable of outlasting physical objects and even planets through constant repair and replication.

Human creativity is "universal" because it allows for mental leaps across an entire idea space, whereas biological evolution is restricted to slow, incremental changes that must remain physically viable at every step.

Learning is not a passive process of absorbing information but a creative act performed by the mind of the recipient. True education should therefore prioritize "coercion-off" environments that allow individuals to create their own knowledge rather than demanding obedience to existing theories.
The content of a person's character and thoughts is primarily a creative product of their own mind rather than a fixed result of genetic or environmental programming.

Science provides a deeper "big picture" of existence than mysticism by offering the necessary context to understand our place in the cosmos while rejecting the limiting idea of an "ultimate explanation.".
summary:
In this conversation, David Deutsch and Naval Ravikant explore the nature of knowledge, creativity, and the distinction between mathematical proof and scientific conjecture. Deutsch clarifies that "proof" is reserved for necessary truths in mathematics, whereas knowledge in fields like science and Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is built through conjectural arguments and criticism. A central theme is the unique power of human creativity, which Deutsch describes as "universal." Unlike biological evolution, which is slow, incremental, and physically constrained, human explanatory knowledge can leap across "idea spaces," making it the most resilient information in the cosmos, capable of outlasting even physical monuments.

The dialogue also focuses on the philosophy of education and individual agency. Deutsch criticizes traditional schooling for prioritizing obedience and the "bucket theory" of mind—the idea that knowledge is simply poured into a student. He advocates for "unschooling," arguing that learning must be a creative, non-coercive act performed by the learner. This perspective extends to the nature-versus-nurture debate; Deutsch rejects deterministic views of genetics or environment, asserting that individuals use their own creativity to interpret and act upon their circumstances. 

Finally, the discussion touches on the future of AGI and the "mystery" of existence. Deutsch suggests that AGI is a software problem of creativity rather than a hardware problem of brute processing power. He dismisses the search for "ultimate explanations" found in some spiritual traditions, arguing that science offers a far more profound "big picture" by continuously uncovering new problems and layers of reality. Ultimately, the transcript highlights a worldview rooted in optimism, where the growth of knowledge is an infinite, liberating process.
